When you're doing audio products and when you're doing video products, it's important to have really good quality audio. People will put up with poor quality, you know, video and even poor quality printing. But when it comes to audio, you really have to have good quality. The good news is that it's pretty easy to do if you just pay attention to a few things. One thing you want to do is watch out for external noises. Often, one is wind if you're shooting video or recording outside. Even a little bit of tiny bit of wind can really destroy it. So you've got to be really careful about wind and just general background noise. Now, it's one thing to have like a siren, like an ambulance go by or a fire truck go by and you have that siren in the background and you can either ignore it or you can make a joke about it. It's not a big deal. But if you've got some consistent noise, you know, someone's using a jackhammer next door to you. And the reason I can say that is because this has happened to me. So there's lots of different types to use. And this is my friend Ed Dale. I heard him use this term, horses for courses. I think I've heard it from other Australians. So let's just call it an Aussie term, horses for courses. That means there's different, if you're going to have a horse race, there's different horses that are good at different types of races. And it's the same way with microphones. There's different microphones that are good for different purposes. So one of them is a headset mic. That's what I'm recording with right now. And the beautiful thing about the headset mic is you don't have to worry about the external environment too much. You put it on, it captures your voice really well. It's very repeatable. So I can sit here and I can record this now. And I could come back, I could stop it right now. And if I came back tomorrow, I could put the headset on and get the same audio quality. And you couldn't tell the difference. I could edit it right in. And that's really cool because one time I was putting together a product and thought it was all fine and dandy. And then so I recorded it, I gave it to my team to edit. I went off on a trip. I got on a plane and I flew a couple thousand miles away. And because I just had this inkling that something might go wrong, I actually packed my headset mic with me. And sure enough, I got to my location and I heard from my team and they said, hey, you screwed this one thing up, you got to redo it. All I did was I put on my headset and in a hotel room, 2,000 miles away from my home studio here, I was able to re-record it. And when it was basically plugged in, when they edited it in, you couldn't tell the difference. 18 minutes of it was recorded in my home studio and 2 minutes were recorded in a hotel room, 2,000 miles away. You couldn't tell the difference. The reason is with the headset mic, you eliminate a lot of the extra variables. You don't have to worry about what's on your desk. You don't have to worry about how close you are to the wall. It's very easy to get a similar sound environment. And it's very easy to get a good sound environment. Another thing you can do is you can get up and you can walk around to the extent of how long your cord is
